title: Error Identification
description: Learn to classify, locate, and fix Java errors like a compiler. CSE110 exams heavily test your ability to identify compile-time errors (syntax/type issues the compiler catches), runtime errors (crashes during execution), and logical errors (wrong output but no crash). This chapter gives you the complete framework.

sections.content: Every error in a Java program belongs to one of three categories. Your first job when analyzing buggy code is to **classify** the error — because the approach to fixing it differs completely.

rows: Missing semicolon, wrong type, undeclared variable
Divide by zero, `NullPointerException`, array out of bounds
Using `>` instead of `>=`, off-by-one in loops

sections.label: Pro Tip
sections.content: In exams, you are usually asked to identify **which line** the error is on, **what type** of error it is, and **how to fix it**. Practice all three steps for every exercise.
The Java compiler is **strict and unforgiving**. It enforces the language grammar and type system before your program ever runs. These errors prevent compilation entirely.

items: **Type mismatch**: `int x = 3.14;` → cannot assign `double` to `int` without cast
**Undeclared variable**: Using `y` before declaring it with a type
**Wrong method return type**: A `void` method having `return 5;`
**Access to non-static member**: Calling `this.field` in a `static` method
**Missing return statement**: A non-void method that doesn't always return a value
**Incompatible types in condition**: `if (x = 5)` — assignment in condition is not `boolean`

sections.title: Compile-Time Error Showcase

sections.content: Runtime errors occur when code is **syntactically correct** but encounters an impossible situation during execution. Java throws an **Exception** object and terminates the program (unless caught with try-catch).

sections.label: Note: Error vs Exception
sections.content: `StackOverflowError` is technically an `Error` (not an `Exception`), but you won't be tested on this distinction in CSE110. Both are types of `Throwable`. Just know it's caused by infinite recursion.

sections.title: Runtime Errors in Action

sections.content: Logical errors are the **hardest to find** because the program compiles and runs without crashing — it just produces the **wrong answer**. There is no error message. You must reason about what the code does vs. what it should do.

items: **Off-by-one**: Loop runs one too many or too few times (`< n` vs `<= n`)
**Wrong operator**: Using `=` (assignment) instead of `==` (comparison) — valid in Java assignment expressions but logically wrong
**Integer division**: `double avg = sum / count;` truncates when `sum` and `count` are ints
**Wrong variable updated**: Incrementing `i` when you meant `j` in nested loops
**Missing `break` in switch**: Fall-through causes unintended execution
**Shadowing a variable**: Declaring a local variable with same name as a field, hiding the field

sections.title: Logical Error — Off-by-One

sections.content: For each exercise below, identify: **(1)** which line has the error, **(2)** what type of error it is, and **(3)** how to fix it. Click **Reveal** to check your answer.

sections.title: Exercise 1: The Semicolon Ambush
sections.error: Logical Error on Line 4: `if (x > 3);` — the semicolon after the condition creates an empty `if` body. The block `{ ... }` on line 5 is just a standalone block, always executed.
sections.explanation: This is a **logical error**, not a compile-time error. The program compiles and runs. But `if (x > 3);` means 'if x > 3, do nothing', and the block below always runs regardless of the condition. This is one of the most common Java exam tricks.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Wrong Return Type
sections.error: Compile-Time Error on Line 6–8: The method `multiply` is declared as `void` but has a `return a * b;` statement. A `void` method cannot return a value.
sections.explanation: The return type `void` means 'returns nothing'. Since the method computes `a * b` and tries to return it, the return type must be `int`. Additionally, `println(multiply(3, 4))` would fail if `multiply` were truly void — you can't print the result of a void method.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Null Pointer Trap
sections.error: This code actually has NO error and works fine. `name` is assigned inside `if(true)` which always executes. However, if the condition were not always true (e.g., `if (x > 0)`), line 7 would be a **Compile-Time Error**: 'variable name might not have been initialized'.
sections.explanation: Java requires **definite assignment** — a local variable must be definitely assigned before use. Since `if(true)` is always true, the compiler knows `name` gets assigned. But `if(x > 0)` is uncertain, so the compiler would refuse. Best practice: always initialize variables when declaring them.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Array Bounds Crash
sections.error: Runtime Error: `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` on the last iteration. The array has indices 0–4 (`length` = 5), but `i <= numbers.length` allows `i` to reach **5**, which is out of bounds.
sections.explanation: Arrays in Java are **zero-indexed**. An array of size `n` has valid indices `0` through `n-1`. Using `i <= numbers.length` means the last value of `i` is `numbers.length` (which is 5), but `numbers[5]` doesn't exist — the valid max is `numbers[4]`. Always use `i < array.length` (strict less-than).

sections.title: Exercise 5: The Silent Average Bug
sections.error: Logical Error on Line 5: `total / count` is **integer division** because both are `int`. The result is `87` (truncated from `87.0`), stored as `87.0`. But if the average were `87.3`, it would silently become `87.0`.
sections.explanation: Even though `average` is declared as `double`, the **division happens first** using integer arithmetic, then the integer result is promoted to double. So `435 / 5 = 87` (int), then `87` becomes `87.0` (double). The fix is `(double) total / count` which forces double division producing `87.0` directly. Try it with `433 / 5` to see the bug clearly: integer gives `86`, double gives `86.6`.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Switch Fall-Through
sections.error: Logical Error: Missing `break` after `case 2`. When `day = 2`, it matches case 2 and prints `Tuesday`, but then **falls through** to case 3 and prints `Wednesday` as well.
sections.explanation: Java's `switch` statement has **fall-through behavior** by default — once a matching case is found, execution continues into all subsequent cases until a `break` or end of switch is reached. Without `break` after case 2, the output is `Tuesday\nWednesday`. Always add `break` after each case unless you intentionally want fall-through.

rows: Null refs, array bounds, division by zero, bad casts
Infinite loop condition, missing update, infinite recursion
Local variable used without guaranteed assignment

sections.label: Exam Reminder
sections.content: When asked 'what is the error?', give: **(1)** the **line number**, **(2)** the **type** (compile/runtime/logical), **(3)** a **clear description** of what went wrong, and **(4)** the **fix**. Partial marks are awarded for each correct element.
